state.name: VGS=0 VDS=0
state.p: Like the n channel JFET, a depletion region is formed by the pn junction between the gate and the channel.
The difference in the p channel JFET is that the channel is now p-type silicon, and the majority carriers are holes instead of electrons.
The gate is more heavily doped than the channel, so the depletion region extends mainly into the channel

state.name: VGS=0 VDS=-2
state.p: Current begins to flow, due to holes being attracted at the drain end. Initially, the relation between ID and VGS is linear. Thus, this is called the linear or ohmic region.
As VGS is decreased, the depletion region extends at the drain end of the channel. This is because the potential is more negative at this end, resulting in a larger reverse bias.

state.name: VGS=0 VDS=sat
state.p: Decreasing VDS will increase ID until a certain point called the saturation point. After this point, increasing VGS will have no effect on ID. This is called the saturation region.
Decreasing VDS further results in the depletion region at the drain extending even further into the channel.

state.name: VGS=0 VDS=-10
state.p: In the saturation region, decreasing VDS has no further effect on ID. ID flattens off

state.name: VGS=0,5 VDS=0
state.p: VGS determines the depth of the depletion region in the channel. The more positive VGS is, the more the depletion region constricts the channel, thereby increasing its resistance.
This is because a positive VGS results in a reverse biased gate-source. The positive voltage at the heavily n-doped gate attracts electrons towards the gate, resulting in a larger depletion region.

state.name: VGS=0,5 VDS=-2
state.p: Making VGS more positive increases the resistance so ID is smaller for the same drain-source voltage, VDS.

state.name: VGS=0,5 VDS=sat
state.p: Since the channel is already more constricted by applying a positive voltage VGS at the gate, a less negative VDS is required in order to reach saturation point.

state.name: VGS=0,5 VDS=-10
state.p: In the saturation region, the more positive VGS has reduced the channel width at the drain end. Therefore resistance increases and the maximum ID is less than it would be if VGS=0.

state.name: VGS=1 VDS=0
state.p: VGS determines the depth of the depletion region in the channel. The more positive VGS is, the more the depletion region constricts the channel, thereby increasing its resistance.
This is because a positive VGS results in a reverse biased gate-source. The positive voltage at the heavily n-doped gate attracts electrons towards the gate, resulting in a larger depletion region.

state.name: VGS=1 VDS=-2
state.p: Making VGS more positive increases the resistance so ID is smaller for the same drain-source voltage, VDS.

state.name: VGS=1 VDS=sat
state.p: Since the channel is already more constricted by applying a positive voltage VGS at the gate, a less negative VDS is required in order to reach saturation point.

state.name: VGS=1 VDS=-10
state.p: In the saturation region, the more positive VGS has reduced the channel width at the drain end. Therefore resistance increases and the maximum ID is less than it would be if VGS=0.

state.name: VGS=2 VDS=0
state.p: If VGS is made sufficiently positive, the depletion region completely constricts the channel.
This happens when VGS=VP, the pinch off voltage.

state.name: VGS=2 VDS=-2
state.p: Once VGSGS is more positive than the pinch off voltage, ID=0 for all values of VDS. The channel is completely constricted, so that no current can flow.

state.name: VGS=2 VDS=sat
state.p: Once VGSGS is more positive than the pinch off voltage, ID=0 for all values of VDS. The channel is completely constricted, so that no current can flow.

state.name: VGS=2 VDS=-10
state.p: Once VGS is more positive than the pinch off voltage, ID=0 for all values of VDS. The channel is completely constricted, so that no current can flow.
